bills.title: Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act
bills.short_title: Protecting Access for Hunters and Anglers Act
bills.stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops three federal agencies from banning lead ammunition and fishing tackle on federal lands and waters where hunting and fishing are allowed. The agencies that cannot make these bans are the Fish and Wildlife Service, Bureau of Land Management, and Forest Service. There are only two exceptions to this rule. First, any bans that already exist can stay in place. Second, these agencies can create new bans only if they can prove with local data that lead use is the main reason wildlife populations are dropping in that specific area, and the state government agrees with the ban.
bills.last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Environment and Public Works.
bills.subjects_str: Hazardous wastes and toxic substances, Hunting and fishing, Land use and conservation, Metals, Wildlife conservation and habitat protection

bills.title: Lumbee Fairness Act
bills.stage_label: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held. Hearings printed: S.Hrg. 119-275.
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives federal recognition to the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina. Once recognized, tribe members will be able to get the same services and benefits that other federally recognized tribes receive, like healthcare and education programs. The federal government will also be allowed to take land and hold it in trust for the tribe. North Carolina state laws will still apply to tribal lands unless the state and tribe make an agreement to change that.
bills.last_action_text: Committee on Indian Affairs. Hearings held. Hearings printed: S.Hrg. 119-275.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subse
bills.plain_summary: This bill would change the rules for buying silencers, which are devices that make guns quieter. Right now, people have to pay extra taxes and fill out lots of paperwork to buy a silencer, but this bill would get rid of those requirements. Instead, buying a silencer would work just like buying a regular gun, with only a ten percent sales tax added. The government would also have to destroy the records they keep about silencers that people already own. States would not be allowed to make their own registration systems for silencers.

bills.title: High Rise Fire Sprinkler Incentive Act of 2025
bills.short_title: High Rise Fire Sprinkler Incentive Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps owners of tall apartment buildings save money on taxes when they add fire sprinkler systems. Instead of waiting many years to get tax breaks for this safety equipment, building owners can spread the costs over just fifteen years. This faster tax schedule makes it cheaper for them to install sprinklers that can save lives in fires. The goal is to encourage more building owners to add these important safety systems to older tall buildings that might not have them yet.

bills.title: Bonuses for Cost-Cutters Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 40 - 0.
bills.plain_summary: This law gives cash bonuses to federal workers who find ways the government is wasting money. Workers can get up to twenty thousand dollars if they spot spending that is not needed. When workers find wasteful spending, their agency must ask the President to cut that money from the budget. The law makes the bonus amounts twice as big as they used to be.

bills.title: Protect Funding for Women's Health Care Act
bills.short_title: Protect Funding for Women's Health Care Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would stop the federal government from giving money to Planned Parenthood and any organizations connected to it. The government currently provides funding to Planned Parenthood to help pay for health care services for women. If this bill becomes law, that money would no longer go to Planned Parenthood, but other doctors and health care providers could still get federal money to provide women's health services. The bill's supporters say other health care providers will be able to take care of women who currently go to Planned Parenthood.

bills.title: Prevent Tariff Abuse Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be sub
bills.plain_summary: The Prevent Tariff Abuse Act stops the President from using emergency powers to put extra taxes on goods coming into the United States from other countries. Right now, when the President declares a national emergency, they can use special powers to control trade and put limits on imports. This bill would take away the President's ability to add these extra costs and limits on foreign goods during emergencies. The goal is to prevent the President from misusing emergency powers to control trade with other countries.

bills.short_title: Lowering Costs for Caregivers Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about health savings accounts and other tax-free health benefit plans. Starting in 2025, people can use money from these accounts to pay for their parents' medical bills. Before this change, people could only use the money for their own medical costs or their spouse and children. Now parents are included too. This will help people take care of their aging parents without paying extra taxes on the money they use for medical expenses.

bills.title: Return to Work Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
bills.plain_summary: The Return to Work Act requires all government agencies to go back to their old remote work rules from December 31, 2019. This means agencies must change their current work-from-home policies to match what they had before the pandemic. The agency leaders have 60 days to make this change happen. Any newer remote work agreements that don't match the 2019 rules will no longer be valid.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps military veterans get business loans from credit unions. Right now, credit unions can only make a certain amount of business loans before hitting their limit. bills.short_title: No Retaining Every Gun In a System That Restricts Your Rights Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about gun store records when those stores close down. bills.title: PURR Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
bills.plain_summary: The PURR Act of 2025 gives the FDA complete control over pet food rules for dogs and cats across the whole country. This means states and cities can no longer make their own different pet food laws, which should make things less confusing for pet food companies. The FDA must review new pet food ingredients within 90 days, and companies can use ingredients that are already considered safe without getting special permission first. Pet food companies can also make certain claims about their products, like saying they are natural or good for pet health, without asking the FDA first as long as they meet certain rules.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: SAVE Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration.
bills.plain_summary: The SAVE Act requires people to show documents that prove they are US citizens when they register to vote in federal elections. States must check that voters are citizens and remove anyone who is not a citizen from their voter lists. The bill lists specific documents that count as proof of citizenship, like certain types of ID cards. If election workers register someone without proper proof of citizenship, they could face criminal charges or be sued.

bills.plain_summary: This bill protects military members who were kicked out or punished for not getting COVID-19 vaccines. bills.title: TEAM Veteran Caregivers Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes changes to how the Department of Veterans Affairs handles caregiver programs for veterans. It requires the VA to put the names of all veteran caregivers in the veteran's health records so they are officially recognized. bills.plain_summary: This law is about a water pipeline project in Colorado called the Arkansas Valley Conduit. The pipeline will bring water from a reservoir to communities in the Arkansas River Valley. The law makes it easier for these communities to pay for the pipeline by reducing how much they have to pay and giving them more time to pay it back. Communities will pay thirty five percent of the total cost and have up to seventy five years to pay, with lower interest rates if they have money problems.
bills.last_action_text: The Chair directed the Clerk to notify the Senate of the action of the House.
bills.subjects_str: Colorado, Pipelines, Water resources funding, Water use and supply

bills.title: Retired Pay Restoration Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for military veterans who get both retirement pay and disability pay. Right now, veterans with disabilities rated less than 50% must give up some of their retirement pay when they get disability money. This bill would let all disabled veterans keep both their full retirement pay and their full disability pay at the same time. The change would apply to veterans who served at least 20 years or were forced to retire early because of their disability.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.
bills.subjects_str: Disability and paralysis, Veterans' pensions and compensation

bills.title: Stopping Adversarial Tariff Evasion Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for where imported goods come from when the government takes action against unfair trade. If a company from an enemy country like China, Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, or Venezuela owns or controls a business that makes products in another country, those products will be treated as if they came directly from the enemy country. This means companies cannot avoid trade penalties by simply moving their factories to different countries while still being controlled by adversary nations. The new rule helps prevent countries from getting around trade enforcement by using other countries as middlemen.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
bills.subjects_str: Asia, Caribbean area, China, Cuba, Europe, Iran, Latin America, Middle East, North Korea, Russia, Trade agreements and negotiations, Trade restrictions, Venezuela
bills.plain_keywords_str: trade war, tariff avoidance, China trade penalties, enemy country businesses, factory relocation loopholes, foreign ownership rules, trade enforcement, adversary nations, manufacturing outsourcing, import restrictions

bills.title: Promoting Resilient Buildings Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Reported (Amended) by the Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure. H. Rept. 119-319.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for local governments to get money from FEMA to make buildings safer during disasters. Local governments can now use FEMA funding to follow either the newest building safety rules or the ones right before them, instead of only being able to use the very newest rules. The bill also creates a new program that helps regular homeowners get money to make their houses stronger against floods, storms, and other disasters. This homeowner program will last until 2028 and can use up to 10 percent of the money that FEMA gives out each year.
bills.last_action_text: Placed on the Union Calendar, Calendar No. 273.
bills.subjects_str: Government lending and loan guarantees, Natural disasters
bills.plain_keywords_str: FEMA money, disaster funding, building safety, homeowner grants, flood protection, storm damage prevention, disaster preparedness, home strengthening, local government funding, disaster relief money

bills.title: Back to Work Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
bills.plain_summary: The Back to Work Act limits how often federal government workers can work from home to no more than 40 percent of their work days. Workers who work from home will have their pay reduced because they will not get certain pay raises that other workers get. The bill requires agencies to watch workers from home using computer monitoring and review their work agreements every year. Government agencies must report to Congress each year about how well working from home is going and what problems they are having. Some workers like military spouses or those with special jobs may be allowed to work from home more often.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

bills.title: Wounded Knee Massacre Memorial and Sacred Site Act
bills.short_title: Wounded Knee Massacre Memorial and Sacred Site Act
bills.stage_label: Committee on Indian Affairs. Reported by Senator Murkowski without amendment. With written report No. 119-72.
bills.plain_summary: This law requires the Secretary of the Interior to give about 40 acres of land at the Wounded Knee Massacre site in South Dakota to two Native American tribes within one year. The land will become a protected memorial and sacred site that belongs to the Oglala Sioux Tribe and Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe together. The tribes will own the land under special rules that prevent them from selling it and protect it from state and local taxes. The land can only be used for purposes that both tribes agree on, and no gambling or other commercial businesses are allowed there.
bills.last_action_text: Became Public Law No: 119-61.

bills.title: No Taxpayer Funding for Abortion and Abortion Insurance Full Disclosure Act of 2025
bills.short_title: No Taxpayer Funding for Abortion and Abortion Insurance Full Disclosure Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes a permanent rule that the federal government cannot use taxpayer money to pay for abortions or health insurance plans that cover abortions. The only times this rule does not apply is when a woman becomes pregnant from rape or incest, or when her life is in danger and she needs an abortion to save her life. The bill also says that federal buildings and federal workers cannot provide abortions. Additionally, health insurance companies must clearly tell people if their plans cover abortions and how much extra that coverage costs.

bills.title: A bill to require agencies submit zero-based budgets.
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires all federal government agencies to create special budgets called zero-based budgets every six years. When making these budgets, agencies must look at all their programs from the beginning and decide which ones are most important. They also have to suggest ways to spend at least two percent less money than they did the year before. The Department of Defense and the National Nuclear Security Administration do not have to follow the rule about cutting spending.

bills.title: Prosecutors Need to Prosecute Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes prosecutors in big cities report information about serious crimes to the federal government every year. The prosecutors must tell how many cases they decided not to take to court and give details about defendants who have been arrested or convicted before. The crimes include murder, rape, robbery, assault, burglary, theft, car theft, arson, and gun crimes. This rule only applies to cities with 380,000 or more people that get certain federal money. bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Counterterrorism and Intelligence.
bills.plain_summary: The Security First Act gives money to help secure the US border with Mexico from 2025 to 2028. bills.plain_summary: The ACES Act requires the Department of Veterans Affairs to work with the National Academies to study cancer in military aircrew members. bills.plain_summary: This bill tells the Defense Department to create a special fund within two years to help military firefighters who got sick from dangerous chemicals called PFAS while doing their jobs. The fund will pay for medical care and give money to firefighters or their families when the chemicals made them ill. If a firefighter dies from these illnesses, their spouse or other family members can still get payments from the fund. The bill assumes that military firefighters were exposed to these harmful chemicals if they worked at military bases where the chemicals were used.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.

bills.title: Justice for Rape Survivors Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a new rule that anyone found guilty of rape or sexual assault in federal court must go to prison for at least 30 years. The law covers different types of sexual crimes including forcing someone to have sex, having sex with someone who is unconscious or too drunk to say no, and having sex without the other person's permission. Before this bill, judges could give shorter sentences, but now they would have to give at least 30 years or life in prison. The bill is meant to make punishments stronger for people who commit these serious crimes.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: Bring American Companies Home Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_summary: This bill helps American companies move their businesses back to the United States from China. Companies can subtract all the money they spend moving their inventory and equipment from their taxes in the same year they spend it. The government will pay for this program using the money it collects from tariffs on goods made in China. The Treasury Department will create a special fund to hold the tariff money and use it to make up for the lost tax money.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: SERVE Our Communities Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: The SERVE Our Communities Act creates a grant program that gives money to states and local governments to help former prisoners get back into their communities after jail. To get this money, states must let judges think about how dangerous someone might be when deciding on bail. The program also helps prevent people who committed violent crimes from doing them again by providing mentoring and training services. The program will get ten million dollars each year for six years starting in 2026.

bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes rules for the groups that plan presidential inaugurations to be more open about their money. bills.title: Water Rights Protection Act
bills.stage_label: Committee on Agriculture discharged.
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops two federal agencies, the Department of the Interior and the Department of Agriculture, from forcing people to give up their water rights when they apply for permits to use federal land. The agencies cannot make people transfer their water rights to the government or make them get water rights in the government's name. The bill also says these federal agencies cannot put stricter limits on how people use water than what their state already requires. The agencies must respect each state's authority to control water rights and cannot interfere with how states manage water usage.

bills.plain_summary: The Trust the Science Act would remove gray wolves from the endangered species list in most of the United States. bills.title: FIR Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Forestry and Horticulture.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for how the Forest Service and Bureau of Land Management handle their land management plans when it comes to protecting endangered animals. Right now, these agencies have to talk with wildlife experts again whenever a new animal is added to the endangered species list or when they learn new information about how their plans might harm protected animals. This bill says they no longer have to have these new talks or restart their planning process. The agencies can keep using their current land management plans without going back to discuss potential harm to newly protected animals.

bills.title: TCJA Permanency Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_summary: The TCJA Permanency Act makes tax cuts from 2017 permanent instead of letting them expire in 2025. The bill keeps lower tax rates for individuals and families, a bigger standard deduction, and a larger child tax credit. It also keeps limits on how much people can deduct for state and local taxes and mortgage interest. Without this bill, these tax changes would end and taxes would go back to higher rates.

bills.title: Disaster Housing Reform for American Families Act

bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a five-year test program where FEMA will work with housing companies to quickly build temporary homes for people affected by disasters. These homes must be built within 90 days after the President declares a disaster and can house up to four families. The homes must be safe and meet building standards to protect people from natural disasters. bills.title: No Tax on Tips Act

bills.plain_summary: This bill lets workers who get tips take up to $25,000 off their taxable income each year, which means they pay less in income taxes. The bill also gives business owners at hair salons, spas, and similar places a tax credit to help them pay the Social Security taxes they owe on their workers' tips. bills.title: Restore VA Accountability Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Oversight and Investigations.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for the Department of Veterans Affairs to fire, demote, or suspend employees who perform poorly or do something wrong. The VA would need strong evidence before taking these actions, but employees would have fewer ways to fight back and less time to appeal. The bill creates special rules for managers and supervisors that are different from regular employees. It also protects whistleblowers who report problems from being punished. These new rules would apply to cases going back to 2017.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Oversight and Investigations.

bills.title: Young Americans Financial Literacy Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a peri
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a grant program that gives money to groups who want to teach young people about money and finances. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau will give these grants to partnerships between schools, nonprofits, government agencies, and banks. These groups will use the money to create programs that teach kids and families ages eight through twenty-four how to manage money, avoid debt, and make smart financial choices. The program will get between twenty-seven and fifty-five million dollars each year until 2029.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Education and Workforce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration

bills.title: No Bailout for Sanctuary Cities Act
bills.short_title: No Bailout for Sanctuary Cities Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the federal government from giving money to cities and states that don't share information about immigrants with federal immigration officials. These places are called sanctuary jurisdictions because they have rules that limit working with immigration agents or sharing details about people's citizenship status. If a city or state refuses to hold immigrants for federal agents or tell them when someone is released from jail, they cannot get federal money that would be used to help people who are in the country illegally. The Department of Homeland Security must make a yearly list of places that don't cooperate with immigration requests.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

The Bison won their nineteenth national title overall and their tenth championship in just fourteen years. The resolution congratulates not only the players but also their coaches, school leaders, and fans who supported the team. bills.title: Expression of Interest Sensibility Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes when people have to pay fees for asking the government to sell them rights to drill for oil and gas on federal land. Right now, people pay a fee just for asking, even if the government never actually sells the land. Under this new bill, people only pay fees if they win the auction to lease the land, or if they were the first to ask about land that nobody else wanted to buy. The bill also says that requests to lease land must stay active for at least five years unless the land gets sold to someone else.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

bills.title: Protecting Homeowners from Disaster Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Protecting Homeowners from Disaster Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes tax rules to help homeowners who lose property in disasters. Right now, people can only deduct disaster losses on their taxes if the government declares it a federal disaster. Starting in 2025, this bill removes that limit so homeowners can deduct any personal property losses from disasters like fires, floods, or storms on their taxes. This means more families will be able to get tax help when they lose their belongings in disasters, even if the government doesn't officially call it a federal disaster.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subs
bills.plain_summary: This bill lets the President punish people from other countries who make the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant in Ukraine unsafe or try to take control of it away from Ukraine. The punishments include freezing their money and property in the United States and stopping them from coming into the country. The President does not have to punish people who are trying to help Ukraine get control of the power plant back or who are providing humanitarian aid. This law is meant to protect the nuclear plant from being damaged during Russia's war with Ukraine.

bills.short_title: Ensuring Veterans’ Final Resting Place Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Reported (Amended) by the Committee on Veterans' Affairs. H. Rept. 119-343.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for veterans and their families to get burial benefits from the Department of Veterans Affairs. Right now, families have to choose between getting a headstone or getting an urn or plaque for their veteran. The new law would let families get both a headstone and an urn or plaque if they want them. bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the federal government from using taxpayer money to support red flag laws. Red flag laws allow courts to order the removal of guns from people who might be dangerous to themselves or others. bills.title: Combating Cartels on Social Media Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Combating Cartels on Social Media Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Counterterrorism and Intelligence.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes three government departments work together to stop criminal groups from using social media to recruit Americans for illegal activities. These criminal organizations use apps and websites to find people to help them with drug smuggling and human trafficking near the US border with Mexico. The departments must study how this recruitment happens and create a plan to stop it. They also have to report back to Congress regularly about what they are doing to solve this problem.

bills.title: SOPRA
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: SOPRA is a law that changes how courts look at decisions made by government agencies. Right now, courts often trust agencies to explain what their own rules mean. This new law says courts must make their own decisions about what laws and agency rules mean, without giving the agencies special treatment. The law applies to all types of agency rules and guidance documents. This means courts will have more power to overturn agency decisions they disagree with.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

bills.short_title: SAVE Moms and Babies Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
bills.plain_summary: The SAVE Moms and Babies Act of 2025 stops the FDA from approving any new drugs that end pregnancies. For abortion drugs that are already approved, the bill creates much stricter rules. Doctors would need special training and certification to prescribe these drugs, and they must give the medicine to patients in person instead of mailing it. The bill also requires doctors to be able to do surgery if needed and to report any problems that happen to patients.

bills.title: Whole Milk for Healthy Kids Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Whole Milk for Healthy Kids Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Reported (Amended) by the Committee on Education and Workforce. H. Rept. 119-142.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about what kinds of milk schools can serve in their lunch programs. Schools will now be able to offer whole milk, reduced-fat milk, low-fat milk, and fat-free milk to students, and all of these can be flavored or unflavored and organic or regular. The bill also says that when schools count saturated fat in meals, they don't have to count the fat that comes from milk. Schools are not allowed to buy milk from companies owned by the Chinese government, and parents can now write notes asking for milk substitutes for their children instead of only doctors being able to do this.

bills.title: Justice for 9/11 Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Armed Services.
bills.plain_summary: This bill is about three men who are accused of planning the September 11th terrorist attacks. It says that even if these men made deals with prosecutors to avoid the death penalty, they can still be put on trial and could face the death penalty. If any of these men are found guilty and sentenced, they must stay in prison at Guantanamo Bay in Cuba. They would have to be kept alone in their cells with very limited contact with other people and could never be moved to a different prison.

bills.plain_summary: This bill helps Native Americans save money on healthcare costs. bills.title: Capitol Remembrance Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the person in charge of maintaining the Capitol building to create a permanent display about the January 6, 2021 attack on the Capitol. The display must be put in an easy-to-see location and include damaged pieces from the building, photos from that day, and a special sign honoring the police officers and other people who protected the Capitol. The display must also honor those who died or got hurt defending the building and the workers who helped fix it afterward. The whole display must be finished and put up within two years after this bill becomes law.

bills.title: Heartbeat Protection Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make it a federal crime for doctors to perform abortions without first checking for a fetal heartbeat. If a heartbeat is found, doctors cannot perform the abortion except in special cases. The exceptions are when the mother's life is in danger from a physical health problem, or when the pregnancy happened because of rape or incest. Doctors who break this law could face up to five years in prison and fines. Women who get abortions cannot be charged with a crime under this law.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.
bills.plain_summary: The Housing Fairness Act of 2025 requires the government to test for housing discrimination across the country by checking if people are treated differently when trying to rent or buy homes because of their race, religion, disability, or other protected characteristics. bills.title: Write the Laws Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: The Write the Laws Act would stop Congress from letting other parts of government make rules that act like laws. Instead, Congress would have to write all the laws themselves with specific details. The bill says that any new laws, rules, or decisions made 90 days after this bill passes must follow this requirement or they will not count. A government office would have to find all the old laws that give rule-making power to agencies and report them to Congress.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes immigration laws to require that most people seeking asylum or arriving without proper papers be held in detention centers instead of being released while they wait for their court hearings. bills.plain_summary: This bill requires the Department of Veterans Affairs to provide mental health care to certain disabled veterans within five days of when they ask for help. bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.
bills.plain_summary: The KAMALA Act is a law that changes how federal housing money can be used. bills.plain_summary: This bill renews and updates a 1998 law that helps the government study and respond to harmful algal blooms and low oxygen levels in water. These problems can kill fish, hurt the environment, and make people sick when they swim in or drink contaminated water. bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: The Censorship Accountability Act lets people sue federal government workers who violate their free speech rights. bills.stage_label: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 15 - 13.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for federal crimes by requiring the government to prove what was going on in a person's mind when they committed a crime. When a law does not say what mental state is needed to convict someone, the government must now prove the person knew what they were doing was wrong. The bill creates a standard rule that says people must have acted knowingly to be found guilty of crimes where the mental requirement is not clearly stated.

bills.stage_label: Ordered to be Reported in the Nature of a Substitute by the Yeas and Nays: 13 - 12.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes new rules to help protect small businesses from government regulations that could hurt them. Before government agencies create new rules, they must study how those rules might affect small businesses and look for ways to make the rules less harmful. Agencies also have to ask small businesses for their opinions before making major rules and regularly check if old rules are still causing problems. The bill makes it easier for small businesses to take agencies to court if they don't follow these protection rules, and it stops agencies from fining small businesses the first time they make paperwork mistakes.

bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would make Washington D.C. into the 51st state called Washington Douglass Commonwealth. bills.title: Federal Agency Sunset Commission Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Federal Agency Sunset Commission Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a group called the Federal Agency Sunset Commission that will decide which government agencies should be shut down. The Commission has thirteen members who must look at every government agency at least once every twelve years to see if it is still needed and working well. If the Commission decides an agency should be closed, Congress must vote to keep it running or it will automatically shut down. The Commission will also check if government agencies are doing things they are not supposed to do and will tell Congress about any new agencies that lawmakers want to create.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on Rules, for a period to
bills.plain_summary: This bill would give Washington DC more control over making its own laws. Right now, when DC passes a new law, Congress has 30 days to look at it and can stop it from happening if they don't like it. This bill would end that process so Congress could no longer block DC's laws. Once DC passes a law, it would go into effect without needing Congress to approve it first.

bills.title: Lady Liberty Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: The Lady Liberty Act of 2025 requires the United States to accept at least 125,000 refugees every year starting in 2027. Right now, the President decides how many refugees can come to America each year. This new law would change that by setting a minimum number that must be allowed in no matter what the President wants. The law makes sure America will always take in at least 125,000 people who are fleeing danger in their home countries.

bills.title: Lumbee Fairness Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
bills.plain_summary: The Lumbee Fairness Act gives full federal recognition to the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina by changing a 1956 law. This means the Lumbee people can now get all the same services and benefits that other federally recognized tribes receive. The government can also hold land in trust for the tribe, which helps protect their territory. North Carolina will continue to handle legal matters on tribal land unless the state and tribe agree to change this arrangement.

bills.title: SHOW UP Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
bills.plain_summary: The SHOW UP Act of 2025 makes federal government workers go back to the same work-from-home rules they had before the coronavirus pandemic started in 2020. All government agencies have thirty days to change their policies back to what they were on December 31, 2019. If an agency wants to let more employees work from home than before the pandemic, they must get special permission and prove it will help their work and save money. The bill also requires agencies to study how the increase in remote work during the pandemic affected their operations.

bills.plain_summary: The Health Care Providers Safety Act of 2025 lets the government give money to hospitals, clinics, and other places where people get medical care. This money helps these places buy better security equipment and make their buildings safer. bills.title: Skinny Labels, Big Savings Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: The Skinny Labels, Big Savings Act protects generic drug companies from being sued for patent violations when they use special labels called "skinny labels." These labels let generic companies sell their drugs by only listing the medical uses that are no longer protected by patents, while leaving out the uses that still have patent protection. A court case made it risky for companies to use skinny labels because they could still get sued, even when following the rules. This new law makes it clear that generic and biosimilar drug companies cannot be sued for patent violations when they properly use skinny labels approved by the FDA. This protection helps keep generic drugs available and affordable for patients.

bills.plain_summary: The Department of Education Protection Act is a law that stops the Department of Education from changing how it works. The Department cannot use any of its money to make itself smaller, fire workers, or change what it does. It must keep doing things the same way it was doing them on January 1, 2025. This means the Department has to stay the same size and keep all the same jobs and responsibilities it had before.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

bills.title: Estate Tax Rate Reduction Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on the Budget, for a period to be subseque
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the tax rates that people pay on estates, gifts, and generation-skipping transfers. Instead of the current tax system, everyone will pay the same flat rate of twenty percent. The new tax rate will start on January 1, 2025. This means that after that date, when someone dies and leaves money to others, gives large gifts, or transfers money to grandchildren or great-grandchildren, the tax will be twenty percent.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on the Budget, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provision

bills.title: Protecting Our Children from the CDC Act

bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
bills.plain_summary: This bill says the CDC must publish all research data about COVID-19 vaccines on their website before they can recommend these shots for kids and teens. The research data must show how safe the vaccines are and how well they work. bills.title: Proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States prohibiting the United States Government from increasing its debt except for a specific purpose by law adopted by three-fourths of the membership of each House of Congress.
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill wants to change the Constitution to make it much harder for the government to borrow money. Right now, Congress can increase the government's debt with a simple majority vote. If this amendment passes, it would require three-fourths of both the House and Senate to agree before the government could borrow more money for any specific reason. The change would only happen ten years after three-fourths of all state governments approve it.

bills.title: Justice for 9/11 Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.
bills.plain_summary: This bill is about three men who are accused of planning the September 11th terrorist attacks. Even if these men make deals to avoid trial, this bill says they must still go to trial and could get the death penalty. bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it much harder for immigrants in court cases to be released from jail while waiting for their hearings. If they are released, they must wear a GPS tracking device at all times and stay home during certain nighttime hours. bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: The Neighbors Not Enemies Act removes an old law that gave the president special powers during wars. bills.title: DEBT Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_summary: The DEBT Act requires the Treasury Secretary to meet with Congress when the government is getting close to its debt limit. The Secretary must explain what special emergency steps the Treasury will take to avoid running out of money to pay the government's bills. This meeting must happen between 21 and 60 days before these emergency steps are needed. The Secretary has to tell Congress how much these emergency actions will cost and how they will be undone later.

bills.stage_label: Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs. Reported by Senator Paul with an amendment in the nature of a s
bills.plain_summary: The Guidance Clarity Act of 2025 requires all federal agencies to put a clear warning on their guidance documents. The warning must be placed on the first page of every guidance document. It must tell people that the guidance is not a law and cannot force anyone to do anything. The warning also must explain that the guidance only helps people understand existing laws and agency rules. This helps make sure people know the difference between real laws and guidance that just explains those laws.

bills.title: To eliminate certain limitations and exclusions regarding defense articles and requirements regarding security assistance and sales with respect to the Republic of Cyprus.
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill removes special rules that made it harder for the United States to sell weapons and provide military help to Cyprus, Greece, and Turkey. Right now, the President has to prove to Congress every year that these countries are doing certain things before the US can help them with defense. For Cyprus, the President has to show they are fighting money laundering and not helping Russian military ships. For Greece and Turkey, the President has to prove that giving them weapons will keep peace in the area. This bill gets rid of all those extra requirements so the US can treat these countries like other allies.

bills.title: SHARKED Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.
bills.plain_summary: The SHARKED Act of 2025 creates a special team to solve problems with sharks taking fish off fishing lines before fishermen can catch them. This team will include fishing experts, scientists, and people from state agencies who will work together for up to seven years. bills.title: REMOTE Act

bills.plain_summary: The REMOTE Act requires government agencies to track how much their employees use computer networks and login systems, whether they work from home or in the office. bills.plain_summary: This resolution creates a new rule for how long the Senate must wait before voting on bills. For every 20 pages in a bill, senators must wait at least one day before they can vote on it. If a bill has extra pages that don't add up to 20, they still have to wait one more day for those pages too. Any senator can object if they think the Senate is trying to vote too quickly without enough waiting time. The only way to skip this waiting period is if three-fifths of all senators vote to override the rule.

bills.title: STEP Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: The STEP Act makes government agencies work harder to stop wrong payments and fraud. Agencies must watch new programs that spend more than $100 million closely for their first four years. They have to get their boss's approval for how they guess wrong payment amounts and write detailed reports about stopping fraud. Agencies must send reports to Congress every year for ten years about how they are fighting fraud and fixing money problems. The law does not give agencies extra money to do this work.

bills.plain_summary: This bill helps married adults with intellectual or developmental disabilities get fairer treatment when applying for SSI benefits. bills.title: Balanced Budget Accountability Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would hold back Congress members' pay if they don't pass a balanced budget by April 16th each year. A balanced budget means the government can't spend more money than it brings in, and spending can't be more than 18% of what the country produces. The bill also says that any vote to raise taxes or fees must get approval from three-fifths of Congress members, not just a simple majority. For the current Congress, members who miss the deadline would have their pay held until they pass a budget, but starting with future Congresses, they would only get paid one dollar for the rest of that year.

This bill would prevent that new plan from being used.

bills.plain_summary: This bill says the Department of Energy cannot spend money from the Nuclear Waste Fund to build places to store dangerous nuclear waste unless they get written permission first. bills.title: Sunshine Protection Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.
bills.plain_summary: The Sunshine Protection Act of 2025 would make daylight saving time permanent across the United States. This means we would stop changing our clocks twice a year in spring and fall. Instead, we would keep the same time all year long that we currently use during daylight saving time. States that do not currently observe daylight saving time in some areas could choose what time to use in those places.

It also limits how many employees can be moved from regular government jobs to political jobs during each presidential term.

bills.plain_summary: The STAND Act stops the government from spending money on foreign aid for 60 days after the President declares a major disaster in the United States. bills.title: Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act
bills.stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill says that if a baby is born alive during an abortion, doctors and nurses must give the baby the same medical care they would give any other newborn baby. They must also take the baby to a hospital right away. If healthcare workers don't follow these rules, they could go to jail for up to five years. If someone kills a baby that was born alive, they can be charged with murder. The mother cannot be punished under this law, but she can sue if healthcare workers break these rules.

bills.plain_summary: The Paws Off Act of 2025 makes a new rule about foods that contain xylitol. Xylitol is a sweetener that can be very dangerous and even deadly to dogs if they eat it. Under this new law, any food with xylitol in it must have a warning label that tells people it is toxic to dogs. The Food and Drug Administration has six months to one year to create the rules for how these warning labels should work. This law is meant to help keep dogs safe by making sure pet owners know which foods could hurt their pets.

bills.plain_summary: This law stops the President from banning fracking across the whole country unless Congress agrees to it first. bills.title: Federal Employee Performance and Accountability Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Federal Employee Performance and Accountability Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a test program for five years that changes how some federal workers get paid based on how well they do their jobs. Workers at higher levels who do really well can get up to 10% more pay, workers who do okay get no extra pay, and workers who do poorly get 10% less pay and extra training. Each government agency has to pick between 1% and 10% of their eligible workers to try this new system. Agencies have to report each year on how well the program is working, and at the end there will be a final report on whether it was successful.

bills.title: Noncontiguous Shipping Competition Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Coast Guard and Maritime Transportation.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the Jones Act, which normally requires that only American-built ships with American crews can carry goods between US ports. The new law would allow foreign ships to carry goods to Hawaii, Alaska, Puerto Rico, and other US territories if there isn't enough competition from American shipping companies. Foreign ships could only be used when fewer than three American companies serve a route, or when those companies don't each handle at least 20 percent of the goods on that route. The goal is to increase shipping competition and potentially lower costs for people living in these areas.

bills.plain_summary: The JFK Act of 2025 requires several government agencies to release all their records about President Kennedy's assassination within thirty days. bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops Federal Reserve banks from paying interest to regular banks on extra money they keep stored there. Right now, banks can earn interest on all the money they keep at Federal Reserve banks, even if it's more than they are required to keep. bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill limits what the President can do during a national emergency when it comes to taxes on goods from other countries. Currently, the President has broad powers during emergencies to control trade and money matters. Under this new law, the President would not be allowed to add new taxes or raise existing taxes on imported goods during an emergency. However, the President could still completely ban all products or certain types of products from entering the US from specific countries. bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a p
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires government agencies to get rid of ten old rules before they can make any new rule that costs money or creates requirements for people, businesses, or local governments. If the new rule would cost more than 100 million dollars per year, the agency must make sure the ten old rules they remove cost at least as much as the new rule. The bill does not apply to rules about how agencies run themselves, government purchasing, or rules that are being changed to make them easier to follow. All agencies must also write reports to Congress listing their rules and saying which ones are too expensive, don't work well, repeat other rules, or are too old.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill proposes changing the Constitution to limit how long people can serve in Congress. Under the new rules, a person could only serve up to six years in the House of Representatives, which equals three terms. They could only serve up to twelve years in the Senate, which equals two terms. The limits would only apply to terms that start after the amendment becomes law. To become part of the Constitution, three-fourths of all state governments would need to approve this change within seven years.

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes how government agencies study the environmental effects of forest management projects on public lands. Instead of looking at many different ways to manage the forest, agencies can only study two choices: doing the proposed forest work or doing nothing at all. bills.title: SWAMP Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Economic Development, Public Buildings, and Emergency Management.
bills.plain_summary: The SWAMP Act would force most federal government agency headquarters to move out of the Washington DC area. States and local governments could compete to have these agencies move to their areas by making proposals. Agencies that are already in Washington could stay but would not be allowed to build new buildings, make major repairs, or sign new leases for office space. bills.short_title: Restricting First Use of Nuclear Weapons Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.
bills.plain_summary: This bill says the United States cannot use nuclear weapons first in a fight with another country unless Congress officially declares war. The President would not be allowed to launch nuclear weapons at an enemy unless that enemy has already attacked America or its allies with nuclear weapons first. If Congress has not declared war, no government money can be spent on firing nuclear weapons first. This law would require Congress to vote and approve before America could be the first country to use nuclear weapons in any conflict.

bills.title: Chinese Currency Accountability Act of 2025

bills.plain_summary: This law says the United States must vote against giving China's money more power in a special international money system run by the International Monetary Fund. The International Monetary Fund uses a basket of different countries' money to create special reserve money that countries can use in emergencies. bills.title: Birthright Citizenship Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would change who automatically becomes a U.S. citizen when born in America. Under the new law, babies born in the United States would only become citizens if at least one parent is already a U.S. citizen, a legal permanent resident, or someone serving in the U.S. military. Right now, almost all babies born in America automatically become citizens regardless of their parents' status. The change would only apply to people born after the law goes into effect, so it would not affect anyone who has already been born.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_keywords_str: birthright citizenship, automatic citizenship, born in America citizenship, citizenship for babies, immigrant children citizenship, anchor babies, 14th amendment citizenship, citizenship requirements, parent citizenship status, born in US citizen, citizenship law changes, immigration citizenship rules

bills.title: End Taxpayer Funding for Abortion Providers Act
bills.short_title: End Taxpayer Funding for Abortion Providers Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would stop the federal government from giving money to organizations that perform abortions or help people get abortions. It would also stop funding groups that give money to other organizations that do abortions. The only times these groups could still get federal money is if they provide abortions because of rape, incest, or to save the mother's life. This rule would apply to any new laws passed by Congress after this bill becomes law, unless those new laws specifically say otherwise.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

bills.title: Daylight Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
bills.plain_summary: The Daylight Act would let states choose to keep daylight saving time all year long. Right now, most states have to change their clocks twice a year, moving them forward in spring and back in fall. States can already choose to stay on standard time all year, but this new law would also let them stay on daylight saving time all year instead. This means states could avoid changing their clocks twice a year if they want to.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: Western Water Accelerated Revenue Repayment Act
bills.short_title: Western Water Accelerated Revenue Repayment Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes a water payment option permanent for people and businesses in western states who get water from government projects. Instead of making regular payments over many years, water users can now choose to pay off their entire water contract early with one big payment or spread it over three years. When people make these early payments, some of that money must go into a special account that helps build new water storage projects like dams and reservoirs. The bill keeps the same rules about where certain payments have to go based on older laws that were already in place.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

bills.title: Every Drop Counts Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Water, Wildlife and Fisheries.
bills.plain_summary: The Every Drop Counts Act makes changes to a government program that gives money to water storage projects in western states. The law allows more types of water projects to get funding, including projects that store water underground or move water to and from underground storage areas. These projects must be able to store between 200 and 150,000 acre-feet of water each year. The law also extends the grant program for five more years so it will run for ten years total instead of five years.

bills.title: Manatee Protection Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
bills.plain_summary: This bill forces the government to list the West Indian manatee as an endangered species. The Secretary of the Interior must put the manatee on the official endangered species list no matter what. Once the manatee is on this list, it will get special protection under the law. This means the government will work harder to keep manatees safe from harm.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be
bills.plain_summary: The ARC Act of 2025 helps people avoid losing their arms or legs because of a blood vessel disease called peripheral artery disease. The law makes Medicare and Medicaid pay for screening tests to find this disease early in people who might get it, and patients do not have to pay anything extra. The government will also create programs to teach people about this disease and set up special programs to help doctors prevent amputations. The goal is to catch the disease before it gets so bad that people need to have body parts removed.

bills.title: Make the Migrant Protection Protocols Mandatory Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Make the Migrant Protection Protocols Mandatory Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules about what happens to people who come to the US border from Mexico or Canada while they wait for their immigration case to be decided. Right now, the government can choose to either keep these people in detention centers in the US or send them back to Mexico or Canada to wait. This bill would require the government to always send them back to the neighboring country to wait instead of giving them a choice. The change is made by switching one word in the current law from "may" to "shall" which makes it required instead of optional.

bills.title: Qualified Immunity Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates legal protection for police officers when people sue them for violating their rights. If an officer makes a mistake while doing their job, they cannot be sued as long as the law was not clear about whether their actions were wrong at the time. The protection applies to all police officers whether they work for cities, states, or the federal government. The bill also protects police departments and local governments from being sued if their officers qualify for this protection.

bills.title: Securing our Elections Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.
bills.plain_summary: This bill requires voters to show photo identification when voting in federal elections starting in 2026. Voters must show a photo ID to get a ballot, or they can vote with a provisional ballot if they bring their ID within three days. People who vote by mail must send a copy of their photo ID or the last four digits of their Social Security number with their ballot. States must give free photo IDs to people who cannot afford them and provide free copying services at government offices.

bills.title: Territorial Tax Equity and Economic Growth Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Territorial Tax Equity and Economic Growth Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people to get special tax benefits when they live in US territories like Puerto Rico and the Virgin Islands. Instead of having to stay in the territory for 183 days each year, people now only need to stay for 122 days to qualify. The bill also changes the rules about which income gets taxed by the US government versus the territory government. It says that income is only considered US income if it comes from having an actual office or business location in the United States, not just from small support activities.

bills.title: Equal Representation Act
bills.stage_label: Ordered to be Reported (Amended) by the Yeas and Nays: 20 - 19.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes how the government counts people to decide how many Representatives each state gets in Congress. Starting in 2030, the census will ask everyone if they are a US citizen, a US national, someone living here legally, or someone living here illegally. bills.title: CURD Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.
bills.plain_summary: The CURD Act creates a legal definition for what can be called "natural cheese." Natural cheese must be made from animal milk using traditional cheese-making methods. Foods like processed cheese, cheese spreads, and cheese foods cannot be labeled as natural cheese. Companies that put "natural cheese" on their labels when their product does not meet this definition would be breaking the law. This helps shoppers know what they are really buying when they see "natural cheese" on food packages.

bills.title: CLEAN Elections Act

bills.plain_summary: The CLEAN Elections Act requires all states to use independent groups of people to draw the lines for congressional districts. bills.title: Miccosukee Reserved Area Amendments Act
bills.stage_label: On motion to refer the bill and the accompanying veto message to the Committee on Natural Resources.
bills.plain_summary: This law makes the Miccosukee Reserved Area in Florida's Everglades National Park bigger by adding a place called Osceola Camp. The Miccosukee Reserved Area is special land set aside for the Miccosukee Tribe of Indians. bills.plain_summary: This bill makes the VA provide fertility treatments to veterans who have trouble having babies and their partners. bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subs
bills.plain_summary: The MEGOBARI Act is a law that punishes people who are hurting Georgia's democracy and preventing the country from joining Europe. The President must block visas and can freeze the money of Georgian government officials and other foreign people who are being corrupt or violent to stop Georgia from becoming more democratic. Family members of these bad actors can also be punished if they benefited from the wrongdoing. The law also requires reports about how China, Iran, and Russia are trying to influence Georgia. If Georgia makes progress toward democracy, they could get more help from the United States.

bills.plain_summary: This bill gives clergy members and Christian Science practitioners a chance to change their minds about paying Social Security and Medicare taxes. bills.title: VALOR Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.
bills.plain_summary: The VALOR Act of 2025 is a law that puts tough punishments on Venezuela's current government led by Nicolas Maduro because they are not running the country fairly or democratically. The law blocks Venezuelan government money and property in the US, stops Americans from doing business with Venezuela's debt, and may cut off aid to other countries that help Maduro's government. At the same time, the law allows the US to help Venezuelan people and groups working for democracy, and it requires the government to make a plan to provide food, medicine, and other aid once Venezuela gets a fairly elected government. The punishments will be removed only when Venezuela has free and fair elections, respects human rights, frees political prisoners, and creates an independent court system.

bills.title: Promoting New Bank Formation Act
bills.stage_label: Reported (Amended) by the Committee on Financial Services. H. Rept. 119-90.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier to start new banks by giving them three years to slowly build up the money they need to keep on hand instead of having it all right away. During those three years, new banks can ask to change their business plans and regulators have to respond within 30 days. The bill also helps small rural banks by lowering how much money they need to keep compared to their loans for the first three years. bills.title: ERASER Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: The ERASER Act requires government agencies to cancel three old rules before they can make any new rule. If the new rule is a major one that costs more than 100 million dollars per year, the agency must make sure the new rule costs the same or less than the three old rules they are canceling. The bill only applies to certain types of rules and does not cover things like employee rules or how agencies buy supplies. The Government Accountability Office must write reports about how many rules exist and how much they cost.

bills.title: SEC Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.
bills.plain_summary: The SEC Act of 2025 changes how financial regulators count violations when companies break securities laws. Instead of counting each separate wrongful act as its own violation, regulators must group together violations that come from the same cause, false statement, or ongoing problem. All violations in each group get treated as just one violation when deciding penalties. This means companies may face smaller fines because multiple related violations will only count as one violation instead of many separate ones.

bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people to get rid of private student loan debt when they file for bankruptcy. Right now, both government and private student loans are very hard to erase in bankruptcy because you have to prove they cause serious financial hardship. The bill changes the rules so that private student loans can be discharged in bankruptcy just like credit card debt or other regular debts. bills.title: Grant’s Law
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: Grant's Law says that when someone who is in the United States illegally gets arrested for certain crimes, the government must keep them in jail even before they are found guilty. The Department of Homeland Security has to hold these people until their immigration case is finished, which must happen within 90 days. If the person is let out for their criminal court case, the government has to take them back into custody right after. Even if the person is found not guilty of the crime they were arrested for, they still have to stay in jail until their immigration case is done.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.title: Preserving Safe Communities by Ending Swatting Act of 2025
bills.short_title: Preserving Safe Communities by Ending Swatting Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it a crime to lie to emergency services like police or fire departments on purpose. It is illegal to give false information that would make them think there is a dangerous situation happening when there is not. This fake reporting is called swatting and it wastes emergency resources and can hurt innocent people. If someone gets hurt or killed because of the false report, the person who lied will face more serious punishment. People who are harmed by swatting can also sue the person who made the false report.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.short_title: Protecting Life and Taxpayers Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
bills.plain_summary: This bill says that organizations must promise not to perform abortions or give money to other groups that perform abortions if they want to receive federal government funding. There are only three exceptions when abortions would still be allowed: when the pregnancy resulted from rape, when it resulted from incest, or when the mother's life is in serious danger. Any group that wants federal money would have to sign a paper promising to follow these rules. This law would apply to all federal funding that goes to healthcare organizations and other groups.

bills.title: FARM Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.
bills.plain_summary: The FARM Act makes changes to how the government watches foreign investments in American farming. It adds the Secretary of Agriculture to a special government committee that reviews when foreign countries or companies try to buy or control American businesses. The bill requires this committee to look at any foreign investment that could take control of American farms or food companies. It also says that farming and food systems are important to national security. Every year, the government must write reports to Congress about how foreign countries are trying to influence American farming and food production.

bills.title: Healthy SNAP Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Nutrition and Foreign Agriculture.
bills.plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for what food people can buy with SNAP benefits, which are like food stamps that help families buy groceries. People will no longer be able to use SNAP to buy soft drinks, candy, ice cream, or prepared desserts like cookies and cakes. The Department of Agriculture will create a list of healthy foods that SNAP users can buy, focusing on foods that have important nutrients and help people stay healthy. The department will review and update this list every five years to make sure it includes the best food choices. States can ask to include different healthy foods on their lists if they match the nutrition requirements and fit with local eating habits.

bills.plain_summary: This bill creates new rules in the Senate that make it much harder to cut benefits from Medicare or Social Security. If someone wants to reduce these benefits, they would need two-thirds of all senators to vote yes, which is very difficult to get. The bill also stops Congress from taking money that was meant for Medicare and using it to pay for other programs that have nothing to do with Medicare. These rules are meant to protect older Americans who depend on Medicare and Social Security.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_summary: This bill raises taxes on large private colleges and universities that have big endowments. Right now these schools pay a 1.4 percent tax on money their endowments earn from investments, but this bill would increase that tax to 21 percent. The tax only applies to private schools with at least 500 students paying tuition and endowments worth at least $500,000 per student. All the extra money collected from this higher tax must go toward paying down the country's debt.

bills.stage_label: Subcommittee on Health Discharged
bills.plain_summary: The Dental Care for Veterans Act would let all veterans get dental care from the VA, just like they can get other medical care. Right now, most veterans can only get VA dental care if their dental problems are connected to their military service or they meet other strict rules. The new law would change this over four years, starting with veterans who have the highest priority for VA care. This means veterans with service-connected disabilities, former prisoners of war, and those who received Purple Hearts would get dental care first, followed by other veterans.

bills.title: Proposing a Federal debt limit amendment to the Constitution of the United States.
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill wants to add a new rule to the Constitution that would limit how much money the federal government can borrow. The government could only owe up to 130 percent of all the money the country makes in a year, and this limit would slowly go down to 120 percent over time. The only exception would be if the country is at war or facing a serious threat to national security, when the government could spend more money on the military. The President would have to make sure that all government budgets follow these new borrowing limits.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
