bills.title: WISER Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: The WISER Act of 2025 helps women veterans who were forced to leave military service between 1951 and 1976 because they became pregnant, had a baby, or became a parent through adoption or custody. These women were kicked out of the military under an old rule called Executive Order 10240. The bill creates two programs to help them: one upgrades their military discharge records to make them better, and another gives them a one-time payment of twenty-five thousand dollars. Women veterans must apply for these programs, and if a veteran has died, her surviving spouse can still apply for the money.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.
bills.subjects_str: Veterans' pensions and compensation, Women's employment

bills.title: ACRES Act
bills.stage_label: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources. Ordered to be reported without amendment favorably.
bills.plain_summary: This law requires government agencies that manage federal lands to report how many acres they worked on each year to prevent wildfires by removing dangerous plants and trees. The agencies must also create better ways to track their work and make sure their numbers are accurate. They need to study whether their wildfire prevention work actually helps reduce fire risks over time. Finally, an independent government office will check to see if this new law is working properly and report back to Congress.
bills.last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources. Ordered to be reported without amendment favorably.

bills.title: SWAMP Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: The SWAMP Act would force most government agency headquarters to move out of the Washington DC area. States and local governments could compete to have these agencies move to their areas instead. Agencies that are already in DC cannot build new buildings or sign new leases for their offices. The government would pay for these moves by using money it already has and by selling buildings.

bills.short_title: Constitutional Emoluments Protection of American Interests Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
bills.plain_summary: This bill would stop the federal government from spending taxpayer money at any business or property owned or controlled by Donald Trump. bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Armed Services.
bills.plain_summary: This bill tells the Department of Defense to make new rules about parental leave for people in the military. bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committees on the Judiciary, and Appropriations, fo
bills.plain_summary: This bill punishes the International Criminal Court when it tries to investigate or arrest Americans or people from US ally countries that never agreed to let the court have power over them. If the court goes after these protected people, the President must block certain foreign individuals from entering the US and freeze their money and property. The bill also stops all US government money from going to the International Criminal Court. The punishments also apply to family members of people who help the court with these cases.

bills.short_title: Proxy Voting for New Parents Resolution
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Rules.
bills.plain_summary: This resolution lets House members who just had a baby or whose spouse had a baby vote through another member for up to 12 weeks. It also applies to pregnant members who cannot travel safely or have serious medical problems. To use proxy voting, members must write a signed letter saying why they need it and which other member will vote for them. The proxy votes count in regular voting but not when counting how many members are present for a quorum. bills.title: Spectrum Pipeline Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.
bills.plain_summary: This law allows the government to sell radio wave licenses to private companies again through auctions, which they haven't been able to do since 2023. It also requires the government to find at least 2,500 megahertz of radio waves that government agencies currently use and give them to private companies or make them free for everyone to use. At least half of these radio waves must be sold to companies like cell phone providers, while another portion will be set aside for things like Wi-Fi that anyone can use for free. The law gives the government eight years to complete this process and requires regular reports to Congress on their progress. When government agencies have to move to new radio frequencies, they can now get money faster to upgrade their equipment.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: Midnight Rules Relief Act
bills.stage_label: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for Congress to reject rules made by government agencies during a president's last year in office. Right now, Congress has to write a separate document to reject each rule one by one. This new law would let Congress write just one document that rejects many of these "midnight rules" all at the same time. This would save time when Congress wants to get rid of multiple rules that were created right before a new president takes office.

bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill creates a 13-person commission to study slavery in America from 1619 to 1865 and how it still affects African Americans today. The commission will look at laws and policies that hurt formerly enslaved people and their families after slavery ended. They will recommend ways for the United States to make up for the harm caused by slavery and discrimination, which could include an official apology and payments called reparations. The commission has one year to finish their work and report back to Congress, with 12 million dollars provided to fund their research.

bills.short_title: Ending Common Core and Expanding School Choice Act

bills.plain_summary: This bill changes how the federal government gives money to schools for poor children. Instead of requiring states to follow federal rules about testing and teaching standards, states can decide how to use the money themselves. The money is given out based on how many poor children live in each state. bills.title: SMART Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: The SMART Act of 2025 makes government agencies create plans to check if their big rules are working properly. These big rules are ones that cost at least 100 million dollars per year or have major effects on things like jobs, prices, or safety. Agencies must review these rules within the time they set in their plans and compare what they expected to happen with what actually happened. They also have to decide if the rules need to be changed, removed, or if there are better ways to reach their goals. The agencies must share their review results with the public.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, and in addition to the Committees on Education and Workfor
bills.plain_summary: This bill cancels two rules made by the President that required COVID-19 vaccines for federal workers and people who work for companies that do business with the government. It also stops government agencies from making new rules that would force employers to require their workers to get COVID-19 vaccines or get tested if they are not vaccinated. The bill specifically prevents the Department of Labor from requiring workplace vaccine mandates and stops the Department of Health and Human Services from requiring healthcare providers to mandate vaccines for their employees. If passed, this law would remove existing COVID-19 vaccine requirements and prevent new ones from being created.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill is about veterans and their right to buy guns. Right now, if the Veterans Affairs Department decides a veteran needs someone else to help manage their benefits money, that information gets shared with the background check system and can stop the veteran from buying guns. This bill says that needing help with benefits alone should not prevent veterans from buying guns. The Veterans Affairs Department must tell the Justice Department that it was wrong to share this kind of information about veterans since 1993.

bills.title: No Support for Terror Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Financial Services, and in addition to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, for a period to be
bills.plain_summary: The No Support for Terror Act stops the United States from giving money to countries that commit genocide or support terrorism. The bill tells the Treasury Department to oppose giving special international money called Special Drawing Rights to these bad countries through the International Monetary Fund. It also requires government agencies to check that U.S. aid money going to charities and international groups does not end up with terrorist organizations like the Taliban. Any organization that gets this aid money must prove they are following U.S. laws that prevent money from going to terrorists.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation.
bills.plain_summary: This Senate resolution asks the National Collegiate Athletic Association to change its current rules about transgender athletes. Right now, the NCAA allows some transgender students to compete on women's sports teams. The resolution wants the NCAA to create new rules that only allow people who were born female to play on women's teams. The senators who support this resolution believe it would make sports more fair and safer for female athletes.

bills.title: Families’ Rights and Responsibilities Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill gives parents the basic right to make important decisions about their children. Parents can choose how to raise their kids, what kind of education they get, and what health care they receive. The government can only step in if there is a very serious reason and they use the gentlest way possible. If the government breaks these rules, parents can take them to court and might get their lawyer costs paid back if they win.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.
bills.plain_summary: This bill lets disabled veterans drive alone in special highway lanes that are normally only for cars with multiple people. These lanes are called HOV lanes or toll lanes. bills.title: FairTax Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.
bills.plain_summary: The FairTax Act of 2025 would get rid of all federal income taxes, payroll taxes, and estate taxes starting in 2027. Instead, the government would collect money through a 23% national sales tax on things people buy. Families at or below the poverty level would get monthly rebate checks to help pay for the extra sales tax costs. States would collect this federal sales tax along with their own state taxes. The bill would also shut down the IRS after 2029 and create new tax agencies, but the whole plan would end if the constitutional amendment allowing income taxes is not repealed within seven years.
bills.last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

bills.title: Equal COLA Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
bills.plain_summary: The Equal COLA Act changes how much extra money retired federal workers get each year to help with rising costs. Right now, workers who retired under the newer system get smaller yearly increases than workers who retired under the older system. This bill makes it so both groups get the same cost-of-living increases that match exactly how much prices have gone up. This means retired federal workers will get fair increases to help them afford things as they get more expensive.

bills.title: DRAIN THE SWAMP Act
bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This law forces government agencies to move at least 30 percent of their Washington DC workers to offices in other states within one year. Agencies must also get rid of at least 30 percent of their office space in Washington DC within two years. Workers who get moved cannot work from home full time anymore, and they will be paid based on their new location. Some important agencies like the Department of Defense and CIA do not have to follow these rules. The goal is to spread government jobs around the country instead of keeping them all in Washington DC.
bills.last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

bills.plain_summary: This bill creates new rules for doctors who perform abortions and abortion clinics. bills.plain_summary: The SAVE Act is a law that controls who can buy oil from America's emergency oil supply called the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. The law says the government cannot sell this emergency oil to companies that are based in certain countries like Russia, China, Iran, North Korea, and several others. This rule applies to all oil sales from the emergency stockpile no matter what other laws might say. The goal is to keep America's emergency oil away from countries that might be considered unfriendly to the United States.

bills.plain_summary: This bill requires government agencies to give early warning before making major new rules that could cost the economy at least 100 million dollars per year. bills.title: Pony Up Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
bills.plain_summary: The Pony Up Act makes the Postal Service pay people back when they get charged late fees because their mail was delivered too slowly. If the Postal Service gets a bill at least 12 days before it is due but delivers it less than 6 days before the due date, or if they get a payment 5 days early but deliver it late, they have to pay any late fees the person got charged. The Postal Service does not have to pay if the delay was caused by something they could not control, like a natural disaster. The bill also makes the Postal Service report to Congress every year about mail delays and requires them to study whether big companies get better mail service than regular people.

bills.title: To prohibit the use of Federal funds to support or facilitate the participation of the Russian Federation in the Group of Seven, and for other purposes.
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.
bills.plain_summary: This bill stops the US government from using any federal money to help Russia take part in Group of Seven meetings. The Group of Seven is a group of seven wealthy countries that meet to discuss world issues. bills.title: .Proposing a balanced budget amendment to the Constitution requiring that each agency and department's funding is justified.
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This constitutional amendment would require the federal government to spend no more money than it takes in each year, creating a balanced budget. The government could only spend more than it earns if three out of five members in both the House and Senate vote to allow it, and total spending cannot be more than a certain percentage of what the entire country produces. Any tax increases or borrowing more money would also need approval from three out of five members of Congress. The President would have to create a budget that does not spend more than the government earns and explain why each government agency needs the money it is asking for. The rules could be ignored only during wars, military threats, or natural disasters.

bills.title: Declaration of Energy Independence Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it much cheaper for oil and gas companies to drill on government land. It cuts the money companies have to pay the government from about 17% down to 12.5% of what they earn from the oil and gas. The bill also lowers the cost to rent the land from $15 per acre to just $1.50 per acre for the first five years. Companies can also get leases more easily without having to compete with other companies, and they can restart old canceled leases at lower costs.

bills.title: Jobs Now Act of 2025
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.
bills.plain_summary: The Jobs Now Act of 2025 creates a two-year test program run by the Department of Labor to help save and create public service jobs. The program gives money to local governments and community groups, with at least half of the funding used to keep workers who might lose their jobs due to budget cuts. The program especially encourages hiring veterans, people with disabilities, those getting unemployment benefits, and workers who lost their previous jobs. This pilot program aims to help areas with high unemployment and poverty by supporting important public service positions.

bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

bills.stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.
bills.plain_summary: The Student Empowerment Act changes the rules for 529 education savings accounts so families can use the money for kindergarten through high school expenses, not just college costs. bills.stage_label: Senate Committee on Finance discharged by Unanimous Consent. (consideration: CR S4321)
bills.plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people to get extra time to file their taxes when disasters happen. Right now, only disasters declared by the federal government can give people tax deadline extensions, but this bill would let state governors ask for extensions when their state declares a disaster too. bills.plain_summary: This bill makes changes to how the government decides which animals and plants are endangered or threatened. The government must put all the scientific information they use to make these decisions on the internet for everyone to see, except for some private state information and military secrets. bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
bills.plain_summary: This bill removes the lesser prairie-chicken from the government's list of protected animals that are in danger of disappearing forever. The bird is currently protected because there are not many of them left in the wild. The bill also changes the law so that the government can never put the lesser prairie-chicken back on the protected list in the future. This means the bird would not get special protection even if its numbers got very low again.

bills.title: RACE Act
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on House Administration.
bills.plain_summary: The RACE Act is a new law that sets time limits for counting votes in federal elections like those for President and Congress. States must finish counting all ballots by 10 PM on election day and announce the final results within 48 hours after the election ends. The goal is to make sure election results come out quickly and people can trust the voting process.

bills.plain_summary: This bill makes all fentanyl-related drugs permanently illegal under federal law. Fentanyl-related drugs are chemicals that are similar to fentanyl, which is a very dangerous drug that causes many overdoses. bills.title: Proposing a balanced budget amendment to the Constitution of the United States.
bills.stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
bills.plain_summary: This bill wants to add a new rule to the Constitution that says the government cannot spend more money than it takes in each year. If Congress wants to spend more money than the government receives, two-thirds of both the House and Senate would have to vote yes to allow it. The President would also have to propose budgets that don't spend more than the government gets. This new rule would not count money used to pay back loans the government already owes. The rule would start working five years after enough states agree to make it part of the Constitution.
